text: Okay, this is going to be a fairly typical sleeve gastrectomy as accomplished using a robot assistant.
The surgeon places surgical trocars into the abdominal cavity, preparing for robotic-assisted sleeve gastrectomy.
The camera provides a view of the normal gallbladder, showing its typical pear-shaped structure and smooth surface.
The surgeon uses a liver retractor to elevate the liver, providing clear access to the upper stomach area for the gastrectomy procedure.
The camera view shows a normal-looking hiatus without any visible signs of a hiatal hernia.
The stomach is visible in the foreground of the surgical field, partially obscured by the omentum.
We'll get that uncovered as the case moves along.
The surgical team places robotic instruments into the abdominal cavity, preparing for the gastrectomy procedure.
A grasper is visible in the surgical field, positioned to manipulate tissues, with additional instruments soon entering the view.
The vessel sealing device is visible on the right side of the screen, attached to the robotic arm, ready for use in the gastrectomy procedure.
The vessel sealing device is used to cauterize small blood vessels in a focused area, with visible flexibility in maneuvering around the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a blade running down the center of the robotic instrument's jaws to cut the tissue held within them.
The surgeon manipulates robotic instruments to work on the greater curvature of the stomach, using a vessel sealing device and graspers visible on the screen.
The surgical team uses instruments to carefully dissect and access the free space behind the stomach, maneuvering around the greater curvature.
That's going to show up just momentarily. There we go.
The surgeon takes one or two bites in the upper or proximal direction along the greater curve of the stomach, using a surgical device to manipulate the tissue.
The surgeon divides blood vessels within the fatty tissue located near the greater curve of the stomach, using precise cutting techniques.
The robotic surgical tool is maneuvered to change direction and proceed downward along the greater curvature of the stomach, utilizing its flexibility for precise navigation.
You hear a little bit of music in the background now.
We like to listen to kind of classic rock in the operating room, so hopefully it's not intrusive for you on this video. We've included it here.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide blood vessels and mobilize the stomach, moving distally towards the pylorus, with the surgical field oriented towards the left.
Now, this video is pretty heavily edited to show just the key points of the operation. The original length of this particular operation was about forty-two minutes.
The surgeon mobilizes the greater curvature of the stomach, moving in a proximal direction, and takes several bites along the greater curve using surgical instruments.
The surgeon temporarily moves the fat pad aside to improve visibility of the surgical field during the gastrectomy procedure.

Robotic instruments are used to manipulate spanning tissue, uncovering the upper part of the stomach. The surgeon continues to use a vessel sealer to mobilize the greater curve of the stomach, progressing around the fundus.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to mobilize the fat at the top of the stomach, carefully working towards the spleen, a delicate area in the gastrectomy procedure.

The spleen comes into view as the surgeon navigates the delicate area where the stomach is closely attached to the spleen. The robotic vessel sealer is used to approach different angles for precise dissection.
The robotic arm slides underneath the stomach to expose the high posterior short gastric vessels, referred to as the 'pocket' in this practice.

The surgeon uses the robotic vessel sealer to delicately dissect the pocket area, revealing a small blood vessel. The instrument's flexible angles allow careful mobilization of the stomach away from the spleen, minimizing traction to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the diaphragm and the hiatus as the procedure progresses.
I've been feeling it since.

The surgeon navigates past the delicate area near the spleen, revealing the hiatus and the anterior border of the left cruce. Tissue is cleared from the front side of the hiatus and the base, ensuring no redundant stomach tissue remains at the top end. The dissection progresses with further mobilization of the tissues.
The surgeon repositions the stomach, which has been fully mobilized along the greater curvature, laying it out in its normal anatomical orientation in preparation for the stapling process.
The surgical assistant, Dr. Alvarez, manually operates the Ethicon surgical stapler to perform the stapling of the stomach, as the robotic stapler technology was not utilized in this procedure.
The surgeon completes the first staple firing at the bottom end of the stomach and prepares to turn the corner at the incisura, ensuring adequate space to prevent folding or kinking.
The surgeon clamps the stapler into place on the stomach and advances a 34 French bougie sizing device into the stomach, ensuring a uniform sleeve formation before firing the stapler.
The surgeon advances the stapler higher on the stomach, moving closer to the visible bougie sizing device inside the stomach, ensuring alignment for a uniform sleeve gastrectomy.

The surgeon uses robotic tools to adjust the position of the stomach, ensuring a uniform narrow sleeve near the top of the stomach. After each staple firing, the surgeon inspects the crotch of the staple line for any leftover staples.

The surgeon examines the posterior aspect of the stomach to ensure exclusion of a large gastric pocket, which appears well-defined. The Ethicon surgical stapler is fired, effectively separating and sealing the stomach tissue.

The surgeon prepares to divide the last bridge of stomach tissue using a stapler load suitable for thinner tissue, positioned near the diaphragm. The stapler is placed to complete the formation of the gastric sleeve.
The surgeon sets aside the excess stomach tissue for later removal and prepares to conduct a leak test on the newly formed gastric sleeve.

The surgeon uses a robotic arm to occlude the flow from the stomach while the anesthesia doctor advances a nasogastric tube into the stomach. The stomach is then inflated under substantial pressure with methylene blue dye, visibly expanding. The robotic arm helps assess the pressure within the stomach.

The surgeon inspects the staple line along the sleeve gastrectomy, checking for any blue dye leakage that would indicate a breach. The sleeve is visually assessed for uniformity and absence of constriction, with the staple line appearing intact and the sleeve maintaining a consistent tubular shape.

The surgeon inspects the cut edge of the staple line for any bleeding. There is slight oozing at the site of the first staple firing where the tissue was thicker. Focused, controlled cautery is applied to the area to stop the bleeding. The remainder of the staple line is checked and shows no bleeding points.

The surgeon uses the robotic needle driver to perform a figure-of-eight suture around a segment of fatty tissue containing large trunk blood vessels. The needle is precisely placed and pulled through the tissue, effectively encircling and pinching off the vessels to prevent clot propagation. The tying technique is demonstrated using a vicryl suture.
The surgeon completes the suturing maneuver, which may reduce pressure on arterial side branches and minimize postoperative bleeding. The suture is then carefully removed from the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon introduces a silk suture to attach the divided tissue to the staple line, aiming to prevent potential twisting of the sleeve post-surgery. The suture is placed carefully along the staple line to stabilize the sleeve structure.

The surgeon removes the suture and needle after completing the suturing. Instruments, including the liver retractor, are withdrawn, and the camera is repositioned to view across the abdomen from the patient's left to right side. A red spot marks a previous trocar site, while purplish mobilized stomach tissue is visible in the foreground. Using a large grasper, the surgeon spreads the muscle and lifts the staple line on the stomach, pulling it through a small incision. The greater curve of the stomach is extracted, and the muscle is closed with a suture using a Carter-Thompson Suture Passer. Numbing medicine is infiltrated into the tissue to minimize postoperative pain.
